ocr.text: In the pursuit of understanding the demographic and health-related characteristics of the sample population, a comprehensive
analysis of various investigated variables was conducted. The data table presents a summary of the basic biological data
collected from 42 distinct items, encompassing a wide array of metrics.
Items (N = 42)  Investigated variables (N = 392)  Used variables (N = 46)
Basic biological data  8  Gender, age, educational and vocational history, approval for long-term care insurance (if available), body height and weight  7  5
Living environment  2  Family structure, condition of house  8  1
Health status  5  Cognitions, mood, visual and auditory problems, need for walking aids  25  8
Medical history and comorbidity  3  Past medical history and comorbidities, medication history  42  3
Locomotive organ problems  4  Complaints, diagnosis for locomotive organ diseases, method of treatment, medical history including fractures or falls  65  3
Physical findings  4  Area of pain (back pain, buttock pain, thigh pain, knee pain), posture classification, neurological signs  19  8
Laboratory tests  5  Serum vitamin D, hyaluronic acid, bone mineral density  3  3
X-rays  2  Semiquantitative X-ray findings related to the spine and knee  159  2

Motor functional assessment  8  Muscle strength, range of motion of hip and knee joints, one-leg standing time, 100-step test, grip strength, lower limb extension power, forward bending, grades of independence  39  12
GLFS-25 score  1  25 items  25  1
Notably, the investigated variables total 392, reflecting the extensive range of factors considered in this study, which include
gender, age, and educational and vocational history. Such diversity in data points is essential for constructing a nuanced

understanding of the population's profile. 

Moreover, the table highlights the utilized variables, totaling 46, which provide
critical insights into the relationship between individual characteristics and their potential eligibility for long-term care
insurance. This information is particularly relevant given the increasing importance of aging populations and the associated
healthcare needs. As the study progresses, these foundational metrics will serve as a basis for further analysis, enabling
researchers to draw meaningful conclusions regarding the interplay between demographic factors and health outcomes. The data
collected not only enhances our understanding of the cohort but also informs policy implications for long-term care planning
and resource allocation.
